summary:
The permanent division of the Roman Empire (395 AD) at the death of Theodosius I between his sons Arcadius (Eastern Empire) and Honorius (Western Empire) was not recognized as permanent at the time — Romans continued to think in terms of a single empire with administrative arrangements — but it proved the definitive split from which the two halves never reunited. The division followed decades of de facto dual administration and reflected the administrative reality that the empire was too large and its frontier threats too diverse for single-man rule.

The Eastern Empire, centered on Constantinople, had significant structural advantages: a wealthier, more urbanized, more trade-connected economy; better defensible frontiers; a larger population; and access to the gold of Egypt and the trade revenues of the Bosphorus. The Western Empire faced barbarian incursions on the Rhine and Danube, had a less monetized rural economy increasingly organized as proto-feudal estates, and progressively lost tax revenue as territory was lost to Germanic kingdoms. See [[event:2790|Division Roman Empire]] for more context. Within 80 years the Western Empire ceased to exist (476 AD), while the Eastern Empire survived until 1453 AD as the Byzantine Empire — a remarkable divergence of fates that shaped the entire subsequent history of Europe and the Mediterranean.
